summary:
The recommended vaccinations for an unimmunized 13-month-old include three doses each of IPV and OPV, three doses of the pentavalent combination vaccine covering diphtheria-tetanus-pertussis-Hib-pneumococcus-rotavirus-hepatitis B, and one dose of measles vaccine as per current pediatric immunization protocols.

Okay, I see your point about combining multiple antigens into single shots—this is indeed efficient clinically—and it’s supported by WHO recommendations which emphasize reducing number of injections while maintaining full protection against major childhood diseases such as those covered under these combinations. This approach ensures timely coverage without overwhelming young children or requiring excessive clinic visits; thus making vaccination more accessible and effective overall across diverse populations globally.

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{A}}$

We are only asked to summarize the *reasoning*, not restate options or answer directly.

Here is my summary:

For a 13-month-old unvaccinated child, core routine vaccines based on global standards include three doses of both IPV and OPV, three doses of pentavalent vaccine (DTaP/Hib/pneumo/rotavirus/hepB), and one dose of MMR-measles component—all aligned with established age-based immunization schedules designed to prevent serious infectious disease outbreaks through comprehensive yet practical dosing strategies.
